question: Does the New Testament repeat the same three-part pattern (gospel call, expose false system, warn of judgment) before Revelation 14? Examine: John the Baptist's ministry, Jesus' ministry, Stephen's speech in Acts 7, Paul at Athens (Acts 17:22-31), Paul to Rome (Rom 1-3).

topics.name: JOHN
topics.entry_text: Prophecies concerning ISA 40:3; MAL 4:5,6; LUK 1:11-17. Miraculous birth of LUK 1:11-20,57-65. Lives in the desert MAT 3:1; MRK 1:4; LUK 1:80; 3:2,3. Mission of MAT 17:11; MRK 1:2-8; LUK 1:15-17,76-79; 3:4-6; JHN 1:7,8,15,22-28,31-34; 5:32-35; ACT 13:24,25; 19:4. Ministry of MAT 3:1-3; MRK 1:4; LUK 3:2,3; JHN 1:6-8. His influence upon the public mind MAT 3:5,6; 14:5; 21:32; MRK 1:5; 11:32; LUK 3:7,15; 20:6; JHN 1:35-40. Testifies to the messiahship of Jesus MAT 3:11,12; MRK 1:7,8; LUK 3:16,17; JHN 1:15,26-36; 3:23-36; 5:32,33; 10:41; ACT 13:25. Baptizes Jesus MAT 3:13-16; MRK 1:9-11; LUK 3:21,22; JHN 1:32. Reproves Herod Antipas on account of his incest; Herod imprisons him, and beheads him MAT 4:12; 14:1-12; MRK 6:16-29; 9:13; LUK 3:18-20. Character of MRK 6:20; JHN 5:35. Jesus discourses upon MAT 11:7-19; LUK 7:24-33. A Nazarite MAT 11:18; LUK 1:15; 7:33.

topics.name: REPENTANCE
topics.entry_text: The burden of the preaching of John the Baptist MAT 3. Of Jesus MAT 4:17; MRK 1:15. Exhortation to PRO 1:23-33; JER 7:3,5; 26:3; HOS 14:1-3; AMO 5:4-6; MAT 3:2. INSTANCES OF: The Jews, by the preaching of John the Baptist MAT 3:6. The Ninevites, by the preaching of Jonah JON 3:5-9. The Ephesians, by the preaching of Paul ACT 19:18.

topics.name: PHARISEES
topics.entry_text: A sect of the Jews ACT 15:5. Hypocrisy of, reproved by John MAT 3:7-10. By Jesus MAT 6:2-8,16-18; 15:1-9; 16:1-12; 21:33-46; 23:2-33; LUK 11:14-54; 12:1; 15:1-9. Reject John LUK 7:30. Reject Christ MAT 12:38,39; 15:12; JHN 7:48. Paul, a Pharisee ACT 23:6; 26:5.

topics.name: GOSPEL
topics.entry_text: Called GOSPEL OF THE KINGDOM MAT 4:23; 24:14. GOSPEL OF GOD ROM 1:1; 15:16; 1TH 2:8; 1TI 1:11; 1PE 4:17. GOSPEL OF JESUS CHRIST MRK 1:1. GOSPEL OF CHRIST ROM 1:16; 1CO 9:12,18; GAL 1:7; PHP 1:27; 1TH 3:2. UNCLASSIFIED SCRIPTURES: ISA 40:8; MAT 4:23; 11:4-6; 24:14; MRK 1:14,15; 13:10; LUK 1:67-79; 2:10-14; JHN 1:16,17; ACT 2:11; 5:20; 10:36; 13:32,33; 14:3; ROM 1:16,17; 10:15-18; 1CO 1:18,21,24,25; REV 14:6,7.

topics.name: PAUL
topics.entry_text: Also called SAUL. From the tribe of Benjamin. A zealous Pharisee ACT 22:3; 23:6; 26:5. Persecutes the Christians. His vision and conversion ACT 9:3-22. Debates on Mars' Hill (at the meeting of the Areopagus Council) with Greeks ACT 17:16-34. Preaches in Damascus, Jerusalem, Antioch, throughout Asia Minor and Greece. Called to be an apostle ACT 22:14-21; ROM 1:1; 1CO 1:1.

topics.name: STEPHEN
topics.entry_text: A Christian martyr. Appointed one of the committee of seven to oversee the daily ministration ACT 6:3,5,6. Faith and power of ACT 6:5,8-10. False charges against ACT 6:11-15. Defense of ACT 7. Stoned ACT 7:54-60; 8:1; 22:20. Burial of ACT 8:2. Gentle and forgiving spirit of ACT 7:59,60.

topics.name: PREACHING
topics.entry_text: Repentance, the subject of John the Baptist's MAT 3:2; MRK 1:4,15; LUK 3:3. Repentance, the subject of Christ's MAT 4:17; MRK 1:15. Repentance, the subject of the apostles MRK 6:12. The Gospel of the Kingdom of God, the subject of Christ's MRK 1:14,15. Christ crucified and risen, the burden of Paul's ACT 17:3. Effective by Peter and other apostles ACT 2:14-41. By Philip ACT 8:5-12. By Paul ACT 9:20; 13:16-43.

topics.name: IDOLATRY
topics.entry_text: DENUNCIATIONS AGAINST: ACT 15:20,29; 17:16; ROM 1:25; 1CO 6:9,10; 8:1-13; 10:7,14,20-22; 1JN 5:21; REV 21:8; 22:15. FOLLY OF: ACT 14:15; 17:22,23,29; ROM 1:22,23; 1CO 8:4,5; 10:19; 12:2; GAL 4:8; REV 9:20. OBJECTS OF: Sun, moon, and stars DEU 4:19; ACT 7:42. Golden calf EXO 32:4. Animals ROM 1:23.

topics.name: WORSHIP
topics.entry_text: To be rendered only to God EXO 20:3; DEU 5:7; 6:13; MAT 4:10; LUK 4:8; ACT 10:26; 14:15; COL 2:18; REV 19:10; 22:8. Of the wicked rejected GEN 4:5,7. Iniquity of the holy things, EXO 28:38. Of angels, forbidden REV 19:10; 22:8,9.

topics.name: PROPHETS
topics.entry_text: Called SEERS. Not honored in their own country MAT 13:57; LUK 4:24-27; JHN 4:44. Persecutions of 2CH 36:16; AMO 2:12. Martyrs JER 2:30; MAT 23:37; MRK 12:5; LUK 13:34; 1TH 2:15; HEB 11:37; REV 16:6.

topics.name: BABYLON
topics.entry_text: Built by Nimrod GEN 10:10. Capital of the kingdom of Babylon DAN 4:30. Prophecies concerning PSA 137:8,9; ISA 13; 14:4-26; JER 50; 51; DAN 2:21-38; 5:25-29. FIGURATIVE REV 14:8; 16:19; 17; 18.

topics.name: WRATH
topics.entry_text: See ANGER. See GOD, ANGER OF. See JUDGMENT. See PUNISHMENT, DIVINE. See PUNISHMENT, ETERNAL.

topics.name: SERMON
topics.entry_text: Of Jesus, 'on the mount,' MAT 5; 6; 7. Of Jesus, 'on the plain,' LUK 6:17-49. Beside Lake Galilee MAT 13:1-52; MRK 4:2-33.

verses.heading: First angel's message — everlasting gospel, fear God, judgment hour, worship Creator
verses.kjv_text: And I saw another angel fly in the midst of heaven, having the everlasting gospel to preach unto them that dwell on the earth, and to every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people, Saying with a loud voice, Fear God, and give glory to him; for the hour of his judgment is come: and worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters.
verses.context: Revelation 14:1-5 introduces the 144,000 with the Lamb on Mount Sion. The three angels' messages follow (vv.6-12), then the harvest imagery (vv.14-20). The first angel announces the everlasting gospel with universal scope.
verses.notes: Greek parsing shows phobethete (aorist passive imperative), elthen (aorist — judgment IS come, not will come), proskynesate (aorist imperative). The aorist of elthen is critical for the anticipation-vs-announcement distinction.

verses.heading: Second angel's message — Babylon is fallen
verses.kjv_text: And there followed another angel, saying, Babylon is fallen, is fallen, that great city, because she made all nations drink of the wine of the wrath of her fornication.
verses.context: The second angel follows immediately after the first, announcing the fall of the false system. The double 'is fallen, is fallen' echoes Isaiah 21:9.
verses.notes: The second angel exposes the false system — Babylon. This is the structural parallel for the exposure element in each NT episode.

verses.heading: Third angel's message — judgment warning against beast worship
verses.kjv_text: And the third angel followed them, saying with a loud voice, If any man worship the beast and his image, and receive his mark in his forehead, or in his hand, The same shall drink of the wine of the wrath of God, which is poured out without mixture into the cup of his indignation; and he shall be tormented with fire and brimstone in the presence of the holy angels, and in the presence of the Lamb: And the smoke of their torment ascendeth up for ever and ever: and they have no rest day nor night, who worship the beast and his image, and whosoever receiveth the mark of his name.
verses.context: The third angel delivers the most severe warning in Scripture — the consequences of false worship. Uses thumos (G2372) for divine fury.
verses.notes: thumos tou theou (fury of God) connects to the OT chemah-thumos bridge documented in 3am-02.

verses.heading: Result clause — patience of saints, commandments, faith of Jesus
verses.kjv_text: Here is the patience of the saints: here are they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus.
verses.context: The result of heeding the three messages — the saints are characterized by commandment-keeping and faith. Structural parallel to Eccl 12:13-14.
verses.notes: This verse functions as the response clause — those who heed the three messages are described by their faithfulness, not just their escape from judgment.

verses.heading: John the Baptist's gospel call — repent, kingdom at hand, forerunner prophecy
verses.kjv_text: In those days came John the Baptist, preaching in the wilderness of Judaea, And saying, Repent ye: for the kingdom of heaven is at hand. For this is he that was spoken of by the prophet Esaias, saying, The voice of one crying in the wilderness, Prepare ye the way of the Lord, make his paths straight.
verses.context: Matthew 3 opens John's ministry with his gospel proclamation. The verb kerysso (G2784) describes his activity. Metanoeite is present active imperative — an ongoing command to repent.
verses.notes: Matt 3:2 parsing: metanoeite = present active imperative (ongoing command), eggiken = perfect active indicative of eggizo (G1448) — the kingdom has drawn near and remains near. This is the gospel-call element of John's three-part ministry.

verses.heading: John exposes Pharisees/Sadducees — generation of vipers, axe at root
verses.kjv_text: But when he saw many of the Pharisees and Sadducees come to his baptism, he said unto them, O generation of vipers, who hath warned you to flee from the wrath to come? Bring forth therefore fruits meet for repentance: And think not to say within yourselves, We have Abraham to our father: for I say unto you, that God is able of these stones to raise up children unto Abraham. And now also the axe is laid unto the root of the trees: therefore every tree which bringeth not forth good fruit is hewn down, and cast into the fire.
verses.context: John's exposure element. He specifically targets the Pharisees' and Sadducees' false security in Abrahamic descent. The phrase 'generation of vipers' (gennemata echidnon) is the exposure formula. Matt 3:10 transitions to judgment.
verses.notes: Matt 3:10 parsing: ede (already) + keitai (present indicative — is laid) shows judgment is imminent, not distant. The axe IS at the root — present tense. ekkoptetai and balletai are also present passive indicatives — fruitless trees ARE being cut down and thrown into fire. This bridges exposure and judgment.

verses.heading: John's judgment warning — baptism with fire, unquenchable fire
verses.kjv_text: I indeed baptize you with water unto repentance: but he that cometh after me is mightier than I, whose shoes I am not worthy to bear: he shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost, and with fire: Whose fan is in his hand, and he will throughly purge his floor, and gather his wheat into the garner; but he will burn up the chaff with unquenchable fire.
verses.context: John's explicit judgment warning element. The Coming One brings both Spirit and fire — salvation and judgment. The winnowing imagery separates wheat from chaff.
verses.notes: pyr asbestos (unquenchable fire, G4442 + G762) parallels the judgment fire language throughout the OT prophets and Rev 14:10-11. The winnowing fork (ptuon, G4425) is a judgment-separation image.

verses.heading: Luke's expanded account — exposure and ethical demands
verses.kjv_text: Then said he to the multitude that came forth to be baptized of him, O generation of vipers, who hath warned you to flee from the wrath to come? Bring forth therefore fruits worthy of repentance, and begin not to say within yourselves, We have Abraham to our father: for I say unto you, That God is able of these stones to raise up children unto Abraham. And now also the axe is laid unto the root of the trees: every tree therefore which bringeth not forth good fruit is hewn down, and cast into the fire. And the people asked him, saying, What shall we do then? He answereth and saith unto them, He that hath two coats, let him impart to him that hath none; and he that hath meat, let him do likewise. Then came also publicans to be baptized, and said unto him, Master, what shall we do? And he said unto them, Exact no more than that which is appointed you. And the soldiers likewise demanded of him, saying, And what shall we do? And he said unto them, Do violence to no man, neither accuse any falsely; and be content with your wages.
verses.context: Luke's version addresses the multitude (not just Pharisees/Sadducees as in Matthew). Luke adds the ethical demands (vv.10-14) — practical outworking of repentance for three groups: people, publicans, soldiers.
verses.notes: Luke broadens the exposure beyond the religious leaders to the entire crowd. The ethical instructions show that John's exposure was not merely theological but practical — exposing specific sins in each group's occupation.

verses.heading: Luke's summary — John's entire message labeled euangelizo
verses.kjv_text: And as the people were in expectation, and all men mused in their hearts of John, whether he were the Christ, or not; John answered, saying unto them all, I indeed baptize you with water; but one mightier than I cometh, the latchet of whose shoes I am not worthy to unloose: he shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost and with fire: Whose fan is in his hand, and he will throughly purge his floor, and will gather the wheat into his garner; but the chaff he will burn with fire unquenchable. And many other things in his exhortation preached he unto the people.
verses.context: This is the critical summary verse. Luke 3:18 uses euengelizeto (imperfect middle of euangelizo, G2097) to describe John's ENTIRE message — including the judgment warnings. Luke labels all of it gospel.
verses.notes: Luke 3:18 parsing: euengelizeto = imperfect middle indicative of euangelizo (G2097). The imperfect tense indicates continuous/repeated action — John WAS CONTINUALLY preaching good news. The critical point is that Luke labels the totality of John's message (repentance call + exposure + judgment warning) as euangelizo. This parallels Rev 14:6 where the judgment announcement IS the everlasting gospel.

verses.heading: Jesus warns about scribes and Pharisees — opening exposure
verses.kjv_text: Then spake Jesus to the multitude, and to his disciples, Saying, The scribes and the Pharisees sit in Moses' seat: All therefore whatsoever they bid you observe, that observe and do; but do not ye after their works: for they say, and do not. For they bind heavy burdens and grievous to be borne, and lay them on men's shoulders; but they themselves will not move them with one of their fingers. But all their works they do for to be seen of men: they make broad their phylacteries, and enlarge the borders of their garments, And love the uppermost rooms at feasts, and the chief seats in the synagogues, And greetings in the markets, and to be called of men, Rabbi, Rabbi. But be not ye called Rabbi: for one is your Master, even Christ; and all ye are brethren. And call no man your father upon the earth: for one is your Father, which is in heaven. Neither be ye called masters: for one is your Master, even Christ. But he that is greatest among you shall be your servant. And whosoever shall exalt himself shall be abased; and he that shall humble himself shall be exalted.
verses.context: Matthew 23 is Jesus' final public discourse in the temple. He addresses the multitude AND his disciples (v.1), warning about the scribes and Pharisees. This section sets up the systematic exposure.
verses.notes: Jesus' opening functions like the initial warning — it describes the false system before the formal denunciation begins. The audience parallel: Jesus speaks to the people about the false system, not to the system's leaders themselves.

verses.heading: Seven woes — systematic exposure of Pharisaic hypocrisy
verses.kjv_text: But woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! for ye shut up the kingdom of heaven against men: for ye neither go in yourselves, neither suffer ye them that are entering to go in. Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! for ye devour widows' houses, and for a pretence make long prayer: therefore ye shall receive the greater damnation. Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! for ye compass sea and land to make one proselyte, and when he is made, ye make him twofold more the child of hell than yourselves. Woe unto you, ye blind guides, which say, Whosoever shall swear by the temple, it is nothing; but whosoever shall swear by the gold of the temple, he is a debtor! Ye fools and blind: for whether is greater, the gold, or the temple that sanctifieth the gold? And, Whosoever shall swear by the altar, it is nothing; but whosoever sweareth by the gift that is upon it, he is guilty. Ye fools and blind: for whether is greater, the gift, or the altar that sanctifieth the gift? Whoso therefore shall swear by the altar, sweareth by it, and by all things thereon. And whoso shall swear by the temple, sweareth by it, and by him that dwelleth therein. And he that shall swear by heaven, sweareth by the throne of God, and by him that sitteth thereon. Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! for ye pay tithe of mint and anise and cummin, and have omitted the weightier matters of the law, judgment, mercy, and faith: these ought ye to have done, and not to leave the other undone. Ye blind guides, which strain at a gnat, and swallow a camel. Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! for ye make clean the outside of the cup and of the platter, but within they are full of extortion and excess. Thou blind Pharisee, cleanse first that which is within the cup and platter, that the outside of them may be clean also. Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! for ye are like unto whited sepulchres, which indeed appear beautiful outward, but are within full of dead men's bones, and of all uncleanness. Even so ye also outwardly appear righteous unto men, but within ye are full of hypocrisy and iniquity. Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! because ye build the tombs of the prophets, and garnish the sepulchres of the righteous, And say, If we had been in the days of our fathers, we would not have been partakers with them in the blood of the prophets. Wherefore ye be witnesses unto yourselves, that ye are the children of them which killed the prophets. Fill ye up then the measure of your fathers. Ye serpents, ye generation of vipers, how can ye escape the damnation of hell?
verses.context: The seven woes form the systematic exposure of the false religious system. Each woe targets a specific dimension of hypocrisy. ouai (G3759) appears 7+ times. The section climaxes with 'how can ye escape the damnation (krisis) of hell (geenna)?'
verses.notes: ouai (G3759, 47 occurrences total) is the prophetic denunciation formula. It collocates with hupokrites (G5273, 20 occurrences) 7 times in Matt 23. The woe oracle is an OT prophetic genre (cf. Isa 5 and Hab 2). v.33 uses krisis (judgment/damnation) + geenna — transitioning from exposure to judgment. v.23 explicitly mentions 'the weightier matters of the law, judgment (krisis), mercy, and faith.'

verses.heading: Judgment warning — damnation of gehenna, blood guilt
verses.kjv_text: Ye serpents, ye generation of vipers, how can ye escape the damnation of hell? Wherefore, behold, I send unto you prophets, and wise men, and scribes: and some of them ye shall kill and crucify; and some of them shall ye scourge in your synagogues, and persecute them from city to city: That upon you may come all the righteous blood shed upon the earth, from the blood of righteous Abel unto the blood of Zacharias son of Barachias, whom ye slew between the temple and the altar. Verily I say unto you, All these things shall come upon this generation.
verses.context: The transition from exposure to judgment warning. v.33 asks how they can escape the krisis of geenna. vv.34-36 announce that accumulated blood-guilt will come upon this generation.
verses.notes: krisis (G2920) of geenna (G1067) — the judgment vocabulary. 'This generation' (he genea haute) marks the judgment as both corporate and temporally specific.

verses.heading: Judgment lament — Jerusalem, your house left desolate
verses.kjv_text: O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, thou that killest the prophets, and stonest them which are sent unto thee, how often would I have gathered thy children together, even as a hen gathereth her chickens under her wings, and ye would not! Behold, your house is left unto you desolate. For I say unto you, Ye shall not see me henceforth, till ye shall say, Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord.
verses.context: The lament over Jerusalem. Jesus' emotional cry shows the same tension between warning and compassion found in the OT prophets. 'Your house is left desolate' (eremos, G2048) announces the judgment.
verses.notes: eremos (G2048, desolate) connects to the OT desolation language. 'Your house' may refer to the temple or the nation. The lament parallels Jeremiah's weeping over Jerusalem (Jer 9:1). Cross-testament parallels for Matt 23:37 found connections to Jer 4:11, 2 Chr 21:13 (OT) and Luke 13:34 (synoptic parallel).

verses.heading: Stephen's gospel history — Abraham called, covenant promise
verses.kjv_text: And he said, Men, brethren, and fathers, hearken; The God of glory appeared unto our father Abraham, when he was in Mesopotamia, before he dwelt in Charran, And said unto him, Get thee out of thy country, and from thy kindred, and come into the land which I shall shew thee. Then came he out of the land of the Chaldaeans, and dwelt in Charran: and from thence, when his father was dead, he removed him into this land, wherein ye now dwell. And he gave him none inheritance in it, no, not so much as to set his foot on: yet he promised that he would give it to him for a possession, and to his seed after him, when as yet he had no child. And God spake on this wise, That his seed should sojourn in a strange land; and that they should bring them into bondage, and entreat them evil four hundred years. And the nation to whom they shall be in bondage will I judge, said God: and after that shall they come forth, and serve me in this place. And he gave him the covenant of circumcision: and so Abraham begat Isaac, and circumcised him the eighth day; and Isaac begat Jacob; and Jacob begat the twelve patriarchs.
verses.context: Stephen begins his defense by recounting God's initiative with Abraham — the gospel history. God appears, calls, promises. This is the gospel element of Stephen's speech: God's faithful covenant initiative.
verses.notes: Stephen's opening emphasizes God's initiative and faithfulness, not Israel's merit. v.7 already hints at judgment: 'the nation to whom they shall be in bondage will I judge.' The gospel history establishes the standard against which Israel's rejection will be measured.

verses.heading: Stephen — Joseph rejected then exalted, pattern of rejected savior
verses.kjv_text: And the patriarchs, moved with envy, sold Joseph into Egypt: but God was with him, And delivered him out of all his afflictions, and gave him favour and wisdom in the sight of Pharaoh king of Egypt; and he made him governor over Egypt and all his house. Now there came a dearth over all the land of Egypt and Chanaan, and great affliction: and our fathers found no sustenance. But when Jacob heard that there was corn in Egypt, he sent out our fathers first. And at the second time Joseph was made known to his brethren; and Joseph's kindred was made known unto Pharaoh. Then sent Joseph, and called his father Jacob to him, and all his kindred, threescore and fifteen souls. So Jacob went down into Egypt, and died, he, and our fathers, And were carried over into Sychem, and laid in the sepulchre that Abraham bought for a sum of money of the sons of Emmor the father of Sychem.
verses.context: The Joseph narrative establishes the rejection-then-exaltation typology. The patriarchs sold Joseph out of envy (v.9), but God was with him and elevated him to save the very brothers who rejected him.
verses.notes: The rejected-then-exalted pattern (Joseph sold, then became governor) is typological of Christ. Stephen is building a case: Israel's pattern is to reject God's chosen deliverers first, then be saved through them.

verses.heading: Stephen — Moses rejected by Israel then sent as deliverer
verses.kjv_text: In which time Moses was born, and was exceeding fair, and nourished up in his father's house three months: And when he was cast out, Pharaoh's daughter took him up, and nourished him for her own son. And Moses was learned in all the wisdom of the Egyptians, and was mighty in words and in deeds. And when he was full forty years old, it came into his heart to visit his brethren the children of Israel. And seeing one of them suffer wrong, he defended him, and avenged him that was oppressed, and smote the Egyptian: For he supposed his brethren would have understood how that God by his hand would deliver them: but they understood not. And the next day he shewed himself unto them as they strove, and would have set them at one again, saying, Sirs, ye are brethren; why do ye wrong one to another? But he that did his neighbour wrong thrust him away, saying, Who made thee a ruler and a judge over us? Wilt thou kill me, as thou diddest the Egyptian yesterday? Then fled Moses at this saying, and was a stranger in the land of Madian, where he begat two sons. And when forty years were expired, there appeared to him in the wilderness of mount Sina an angel of the Lord in a flame of fire in a bush. When Moses saw it, he wondered at the sight: and as he drew near to behold it, the voice of the Lord came unto him, Saying, I am the God of thy fathers, the God of Abraham, and the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob. Then Moses trembled, and durst not behold. Then said the Lord to him, Put off thy shoes from thy feet: for the place where thou standest is holy ground. I have seen, I have seen the affliction of my people which is in Egypt, and I have heard their groaning, and am come down to deliver them. And now come, I will send thee into Egypt. This Moses whom they refused, saying, Who made thee a ruler and a judge? the same did God send to be a ruler and a deliverer by the hand of the angel which appeared to him in the bush. He brought them out, after that he had shewed wonders and signs in the land of Egypt, and in the Red sea, and in the wilderness forty years.
verses.context: The Moses cycle repeats the Joseph pattern more explicitly: Israel refuses Moses (v.27, 35), but God sends this same Moses as ruler and deliverer. v.35 emphasizes: 'This Moses whom they refused... the same did God send.'
verses.notes: The repetition of the rejection-exaltation pattern strengthens Stephen's argument: Israel consistently rejected God's chosen deliverers. This builds the exposure element — Israel's pattern of opposing God's messengers.

verses.heading: Stephen — Israel turned to idolatry despite Moses' ministry
verses.kjv_text: This is that Moses, which said unto the children of Israel, A prophet shall the Lord your God raise up unto you of your brethren, like unto me; him shall ye hear. This is he, that was in the church in the wilderness with the angel which spake to him in the mount Sina, and with our fathers: who received the lively oracles to give unto us: To whom our fathers would not obey, but thrust him from them, and in their hearts turned back again into Egypt, Saying unto Aaron, Make us gods to go before us: for as for this Moses, which brought us out of the land of Egypt, we wot not what is become of him. And they made a calf in those days, and offered sacrifice unto the idol, and rejoiced in the works of their own hands. Then God turned, and gave them up to worship the host of heaven; as it is written in the book of the prophets, O ye house of Israel, have ye offered to me slain beasts and sacrifices by the space of forty years in the wilderness? Yea, ye took up the tabernacle of Moloch, and the star of your god Remphan, figures which ye made to worship them: and I will carry you away beyond Babylon.
verses.context: Stephen exposes Israel's idolatry despite receiving the living oracles. The golden calf (v.41), worship of host of heaven (v.42), Moloch and Remphan (v.43). The phrase 'God gave them up' (v.42) parallels Romans 1:24,26,28.
verses.notes: eidolon (G1497) in v.41 — 'offered sacrifice unto the idol.' The parallel with Romans 1:24-28 is significant: both Stephen and Paul describe God 'giving up' those who persist in idolatry. v.43 ends with 'beyond Babylon' — the judgment consequence.

verses.heading: Stephen — temple theology exposed, God not confined to buildings
verses.kjv_text: Our fathers had the tabernacle of witness in the wilderness, as he had appointed, speaking unto Moses, that he should make it according to the fashion that he had seen. Which also our fathers that came after brought in with Jesus into the possession of the Gentiles, whom God drave out before the face of our fathers, unto the days of David; Who found favour before God, and desired to find a tabernacle for the God of Jacob. But Solomon built him an house. Howbeit the most High dwelleth not in temples made with hands; as saith the prophet, Heaven is my throne, and earth is my footstool: what house will ye build me? saith the Lord: or what is the place of my rest? Hath not my hand made all these things?
verses.context: Stephen's theological exposure: the Most High does not dwell in temples made with hands (cheiropoietos, G5499). This directly challenges the Sanhedrin's temple theology — the very charge against him (Acts 6:13-14).
verses.notes: cheiropoietos (G5499, made with hands) — used in Acts 7:48 and paralleled in Acts 17:24 where Paul says the same about Athenian temples. Stephen and Paul independently expose the same error: God cannot be contained in human structures. This is the specific false-system exposure for Stephen's audience.

verses.heading: Stephen's judgment indictment — stiff-necked, resist Holy Spirit, betrayers
verses.kjv_text: Ye stiffnecked and uncircumcised in heart and ears, ye do always resist the Holy Ghost: as your fathers did, so do ye. Which of the prophets have not your fathers persecuted? and they have slain them which shewed before of the coming of the Just One; of whom ye have been now the betrayers and murderers: Who have received the law by the disposition of angels, and have not kept it.
verses.context: Stephen's climactic judgment indictment. He turns from defendant to prosecutor, echoing the OT covenant lawsuit tradition (riyb pattern). sklerotrochelos (G4644, stiff-necked) is a hapax in NT but appears 5x in LXX.

greek_parsing.gloss: ἀπερίτμητοι          ἀπερίτμητος     G564     [A-VPM] Voc Pl M
τῷ                   ὁ               G3588    [T-DSN] Dat Sg N
οἱ                   ὁ               G3588    [T-NPM] Nom Pl M

verses.notes: Parsing: sklerotrochelos (G4644, vocative plural) — hapax in NT. antipiptetete (G496, present active indicative 2nd plural) — 'ye do always resist' — the present tense indicates ongoing, continuous resistance. This parallels the Deuteronomic covenant language (Deut 9:6 LXX uses sklerotrochelos for Israel). Stephen invokes the full covenant lawsuit tradition documented in 3am-02. The accusation culminates: betrayers and murderers of the Just One, law received but not kept.

verses.heading: Paul at Athens — Creator God who made the world, does not dwell in temples
verses.kjv_text: Then Paul stood in the midst of Mars' hill, and said, Ye men of Athens, I perceive that in all things ye are too superstitious. For as I passed by, and beheld your devotions, I found an altar with this inscription, TO THE UNKNOWN GOD. Whom therefore ye ignorantly worship, him declare I unto you. God that made the world and all things therein, seeing that he is Lord of heaven and earth, dwelleth not in temples made with hands; Neither is worshipped with men's hands, as though he needed any thing, seeing he giveth to all life, and breath, and all things;
verses.context: Paul's gospel proclamation at Athens. He begins with creation theology — God made the world (kosmos, G2889) and all things therein. The Creator-worship language parallels Rev 14:7 ('worship him that made heaven, and earth').
verses.notes: Acts 17:24 parallels Rev 14:7 in Creator worship. 'Dwelleth not in temples made with hands' directly echoes Stephen (Acts 7:48). The Parsons chiasm places this as the B element: Creator God as true object of worship.

verses.heading: Paul at Athens — God's universal sovereignty over nations
verses.kjv_text: And hath made of one blood all nations of men for to dwell on all the face of the earth, and hath determined the times before appointed, and the bounds of their habitation; That they should seek the Lord, if haply they might feel after him, and find him, though he be not far from every one of us: For in him we live, and move, and have our being; as certain also of your own poets have said, For we are also his offspring.
verses.context: The center of Parsons' chiasm (C element): the relationship between humanity and God. Universal scope — 'all nations' parallels Rev 14:6 'every nation.'
verses.notes: panta ethne (all nations) — ethnos (G1484, 162 occurrences). The universal scope parallels Rev 14:6 'panti ethnei' (every nation). Paul uses the Athenians' own poets to establish common ground before the turn to judgment.

verses.heading: Paul at Athens — idolatry exposed, judgment announced, repentance commanded
verses.kjv_text: Forasmuch then as we are the offspring of God, we ought not to think that the Godhead is like unto gold, or silver, or stone, graven by art and man's device. And the times of this ignorance God winked at; but now commandeth all men every where to repent: Because he hath appointed a day, in the which he will judge the world in righteousness by that man whom he hath ordained; whereof he hath given assurance unto all men, in that he hath raised him from the dead.
verses.context: The climax of Paul's Areopagus sermon. Three movements compressed: (1) idolatry exposure (v.29), (2) repentance command (v.30), (3) judgment announcement (v.31). This is the three-part pattern in three verses.
verses.notes: Critical parsing: hupereidon (G5237, aorist participle — God having overlooked) vs. apangellei (present indicative — now commands) vs. estesen (G2476, aorist indicative — appointed) hemeran (day) vs. mellei (G3195, present — is about to) krinein (judge). The temporal shift: God previously overlooked ignorance (aorist), NOW commands repentance (present), BECAUSE he appointed (aorist) a day of judgment. The krino (G2919) + oikoumene (the inhabited world) parallels the universal scope. estesen hemeran (appointed a day) — aorist: the day is already set, judgment is certain.

verses.heading: Gospel declaration — power of God unto salvation, righteousness revealed
verses.kjv_text: For I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ: for it is the power of God unto salvation to every one that believeth; to the Jew first, and also to the Greek. For therein is the righteousness of God revealed from faith to faith: as it is written, The just shall live by faith.
verses.context: Romans 1:16-17 is Paul's thesis statement for the entire epistle. euangelion (G2098) is the power (dynamis, G1411) of God unto salvation. This is the gospel-declaration element.
verses.notes: euangelion (G2098, 77 occurrences) + dynamis (G1411, 118 occurrences) — the gospel as God's saving power. Universal scope: 'to every one that believeth; to the Jew first, and also to the Greek' parallels Rev 14:6 'every nation.'

verses.heading: Gentile corruption exposed — knew God but glorified him not, idolatry
verses.kjv_text: For the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of men, who hold the truth in unrighteousness; Because that which may be known of God is manifest in them; for God hath shewed it unto them. For the invisible things of him from the creation of the world are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made, even his eternal power and Godhead; so that they are without excuse: Because that, when they knew God, they glorified him not as God, neither were thankful; but became vain in their imaginations, and their foolish heart was darkened. Professing themselves to be wise, they became fools, And changed the glory of the uncorruptible God into an image made like to corruptible man, and to birds, and fourfooted beasts, and creeping things. Wherefore God also gave them up to uncleanness through the lusts of their own hearts, to dishonour their own bodies between themselves: Who changed the truth of God into a lie, and worshipped and served the creature more than the Creator, who is blessed for ever. Amen.
verses.context: The exposure of Gentile corruption. orge theou (wrath of God) is apokalyptetai (present passive — IS BEING revealed). The progression: knew God → glorified him not → became fools → idolatry → God gave them up.

greek_parsing.gloss: ὀργὴ                 ὀργή            G3709    [N-NSF] Nom Sg F
οὐρανοῦ              οὐρανός         G3772    [N-GSM] Gen Sg M
πᾶσαν                πᾶς             G3956    [A-ASF] Acc Sg F
ἀδικίαν              ἀδικία          G93      [N-ASF] Acc Sg F
ἀδικίᾳ               ἀδικία          G93      [N-DSF] Dat Sg F
Pres Act Ptcp Gen Pl M

verses.notes: Parsing: apokalyptetai (G601, present passive indicative) — wrath IS BEING revealed (present tense, ongoing). katechonton (G2722, present active participle) — who ARE suppressing truth. v.23 'changed the glory (doxa, G1391) of the incorruptible God into an image' — antiparallel to Rev 14:7 'give glory to him.' v.25 esebasthesan (from sebomai) + latreuo (worshipped and served) the creature rather than the Creator. The exposure: false worship of creation instead of Creator.

verses.heading: Consequences of false worship — God gave them up
verses.kjv_text: For this cause God gave them up unto vile affections: for even their women did change the natural use into that which is against nature: And likewise also the men, leaving the natural use of the woman, burned in their lust one toward another; men with men working that which is unseemly, and receiving in themselves that recompence of their error which was meet. And even as they did not like to retain God in their knowledge, God gave them over to a reprobate mind, to do those things which are not convenient; Being filled with all unrighteousness, fornication, wickedness, covetousness, maliciousness; full of envy, murder, debate, deceit, malignity; whisperers, Backbiters, haters of God, despiteful, proud, boasters, inventors of evil things, disobedient to parents, Without understanding, covenantbreakers, without natural affection, implacable, unmerciful: Who knowing the judgment of God, that they which commit such things are worthy of death, not only do the same, but have pleasure in them that do them.
verses.context: The consequences of the false worship exposed in vv.18-25. Three-fold 'God gave them up' (vv.24, 26, 28) shows judgment as the natural consequence of persistent rebellion.
verses.notes: The triple paredoken (gave them up) parallels Acts 7:42 where God 'gave them up to worship the host of heaven.' Both Paul and Stephen describe God's response to persistent idolatry as judicial abandonment.

verses.heading: Jewish hypocrisy exposed — thou that judgest doest the same things
verses.kjv_text: Therefore thou art inexcusable, O man, whosoever thou art that judgest: for wherein thou judgest another, thou condemnest thyself; for thou that judgest doest the same things. But we are sure that the judgment of God is according to truth against them which commit such things. And thinkest thou this, O man, that judgest them which do such things, and doest the same, that thou shalt escape the judgment of God? Or despisest thou the riches of his goodness and forbearance and longsuffering; not knowing that the goodness of God leadeth thee to repentance? But after thy hardness and impenitent heart treasurest up unto thyself wrath against the day of wrath and revelation of the righteous judgment of God; Who will render to every man according to his deeds: To them who by patient continuance in well doing seek for glory and honour and immortality, eternal life: But unto them that are contentious, and do not obey the truth, but obey unrighteousness, indignation and wrath, Tribulation and anguish, upon every soul of man that doeth evil, of the Jew first, and also of the Gentile; But glory, honour, and peace, to every man that worketh good, to the Jew first, and also to the Gentile: For there is no respect of persons with God.
verses.context: Paul turns from Gentile to Jewish corruption. The exposure now targets the one who judges others while doing the same. This parallels Jesus' exposure of Pharisaic hypocrisy in Matt 23.
verses.notes: v.5: orge (G3709) + hemera (G2250) — 'day of wrath' — eschatological judgment language. v.4: metanoia (G3341) — 'repentance.' Paul's exposure of Jewish hypocrisy uses the same pattern as Jesus in Matt 23: you condemn others for what you do yourselves.

verses.heading: Judgment according to gospel — God judges secrets by Jesus Christ
verses.kjv_text: For as many as have sinned without law shall also perish without law: and as many as have sinned in the law shall be judged by the law; (For not the hearers of the law are just before God, but the doers of the law shall be justified. For when the Gentiles, which have not the law, do by nature the things contained in the law, these, having not the law, are a law unto themselves: Which shew the work of the law written in their hearts, their conscience also bearing witness, and their thoughts the mean while accusing or else excusing one another;) In the day when God shall judge the secrets of men by Jesus Christ according to my gospel.
verses.context: The critical verse linking judgment to gospel. Paul explicitly says judgment happens 'according to MY gospel' (kata to euangelion mou). This proves judgment IS gospel, not separate from it.
verses.notes: Parsing: krinei (G2919, present active indicative — or futuristic present: God judges/shall judge) ta krypta (the hidden things) ton anthropon (of men) kata to euangelion mou (according to my gospel) dia Christou Iesou (through Christ Jesus). The present tense krinei may be futuristic or gnomic — describing what God characteristically does. The key phrase: kata to euangelion mou — judgment IS according to/part of the gospel. This parallels Rev 14:6-7 where the judgment announcement IS the everlasting gospel.

verses.heading: Universal judgment — all under sin, every mouth stopped
verses.kjv_text: What then? are we better than they? No, in no wise: for we have before proved both Jews and Gentiles, that they are all under sin; As it is written, There is none righteous, no, not one: There is none that understandeth, there is none that seeketh after God. They are all gone out of the way, they are together become unprofitable; there is none that doeth good, no, not one. Their throat is an open sepulchre; with their tongues they have used deceit; the poison of asps is under their lips: Whose mouth is full of cursing and bitterness: Their feet are swift to shed blood: Destruction and misery are in their ways: And the way of peace have they not known: There is no fear of God before their eyes. Now we know that what things soever the law saith, it saith to them who are under the law: that every mouth may be stopped, and all the world may become guilty before God. Therefore by the deeds of the law there shall no flesh be justified in his sight: for by the law is the knowledge of sin.
verses.context: The judgment verdict. After exposing both Gentile and Jewish corruption, Paul pronounces the universal sentence: 'all the world guilty before God' (v.19). This is the judgment-warning element of Romans 1-3.
verses.notes: v.19 hupodikos (under judgment) — the entire world stands under God's judicial verdict. v.18 'no fear of God before their eyes' inverts Rev 14:7's 'Fear God.' The catena of OT quotations (vv.10-18) functions like a covenant-lawsuit evidence presentation.

verses.heading: Compressed three-part pattern — turned to God, from idols, wrath to come
verses.kjv_text: For they themselves shew of us what manner of entering in we had unto you, and how ye turned to God from idols to serve the living and true God; And to wait for his Son from heaven, whom he raised from the dead, even Jesus, which delivered us from the wrath to come.
verses.context: Paul summarizes his Thessalonian preaching in a compressed three-element formula: (1) turned to God (gospel), (2) from idols (exposure/separation from false system), (3) wait for Son/wrath to come (judgment expectation).

greek_parsing.gloss: τῶν                  ὁ               G3588    [T-GPN] Gen Pl N
Pres Act Ptcp Dat Sg M

verses.notes: Parsing: epestrepsate (G1994, aorist active — turned, completed action) pros ton Theon (to God) apo ton eidolon (from the idols, G1497) douleuein (present infinitive — to serve continuously) anamanein (present infinitive — to await continuously). Three temporal layers: aorist (decisive turning), present (ongoing service), present (ongoing waiting). The three elements map directly to the three angels: gospel (turned to God) = first angel; from idols (exposure) = second angel (Babylon fallen); wrath to come (judgment) = third angel.

verses.heading: Paul before Felix — righteousness, temperance, judgment to come
verses.kjv_text: And after certain days, when Felix came with his wife Drusilla, which was a Jewess, he sent for Paul, and heard him concerning the faith in Christ. And as he reasoned of righteousness, temperance, and judgment to come, Felix trembled, and answered, Go thy way for this time; when I have a convenient season, I will call for thee.
verses.context: Paul's preaching before Felix compresses the pattern into three topics: righteousness (gospel standard), temperance (exposure of Felix's moral failures), judgment to come (the warning). Felix trembled — the response of conviction.
verses.notes: krima to mellon (the coming judgment) — future participle. This is judgment as anticipated/future, not arrived — contrasting with Rev 14:7's elthen (aorist — IS come). Andrews' anticipation-vs-announcement distinction.

verses.heading: OT Elijah prophecy — forerunner before great and dreadful day
verses.kjv_text: Behold, I will send you Elijah the prophet before the coming of the great and dreadful day of the LORD: And he shall turn the heart of the fathers to the children, and the heart of the children to their fathers, lest I come and smite the earth with a curse.
verses.context: The closing prophecy of the OT — God will send Elijah before the day of the LORD. Applied to John the Baptist (Matt 11:14, 17:12-13) and to end-time ministry. The typological backbone of the three Elijahs framework.
verses.notes: This is the bridge verse between OT and NT. The Elijah coming before judgment maps to the pattern: the forerunner delivers the message (gospel + exposure + warning) before God acts in judgment.

verses.heading: Prophecy of the forerunner — voice crying in wilderness
verses.kjv_text: The voice of him that crieth in the wilderness, Prepare ye the way of the LORD, make straight in the desert a highway for our God.
verses.context: Applied to John the Baptist in all four Gospels (Matt 3:3, Mark 1:3, Luke 3:4, John 1:23). The forerunner prepares the way — the gospel-call function.
verses.notes: The wilderness setting connects to the prophetic confrontation tradition. The forerunner's role is proclamation — preparing for God's coming, which includes judgment.

verses.heading: OT structural match — fear God, commandments, judgment
verses.kjv_text: Let us hear the conclusion of the whole matter: Fear God, and keep his commandments: for this is the whole duty of man. For God shall bring every work into judgment, with every secret thing, whether it be good, or whether it be evil.
verses.context: Solomon's conclusion provides an exact structural parallel to Rev 14:7,12: fear God (Rev 14:7a), keep commandments (Rev 14:12), judgment on every work (Rev 14:7b).
verses.notes: The LXX uses phobou ton theon (fear God) — the same construction as Rev 14:7 phobethete ton theon. The 'every secret thing' parallels Rom 2:16 'God shall judge the secrets of men.' This OT-NT bridge was documented in gsp-10.

verses.heading: Basar/euangelizo bridge — good tidings in context of Babylon deliverance
verses.kjv_text: How beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him that bringeth good tidings, that publisheth peace; that bringeth good tidings of good, that publisheth salvation; that saith unto Zion, Thy God reigneth!
verses.context: The OT gospel herald. Paul quotes this in Rom 10:15 as gospel. The context is deliverance FROM Babylon (Isa 52:11 — 'go ye out from thence'). Babylon's fall IS gospel.
verses.notes: basar (H1319) → euangelizo (G2097) through the LXX bridge documented in TR-basar-euangelizo. The context is critical: the good tidings are proclaimed in the setting of deliverance from Babylon — paralleling the second angel's message.

verses.heading: Peter's Pentecost sermon — gospel proclamation, exposure, repentance call
verses.kjv_text: But Peter, standing up with the eleven, lifted up his voice, and said unto them, Ye men of Judaea, and all ye that dwell at Jerusalem, be this known unto you, and hearken to my words: For these are not drunken, as ye suppose, seeing it is but the third hour of the day. But this is that which was spoken by the prophet Joel; And it shall come to pass in the last days, saith God, I will pour out of my Spirit upon all flesh... Ye men of Israel, hear these words; Jesus of Nazareth, a man approved of God among you by miracles and wonders and signs, which God did by him in the midst of you, as ye yourselves also know: Him, being delivered by the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God, ye have taken, and by wicked hands have crucified and slain: Whom God hath raised up... Therefore let all the house of Israel know assuredly, that God hath made that same Jesus, whom ye have crucified, both Lord and Christ. Now when they heard this, they were pricked in their heart, and said unto Peter and to the rest of the apostles, Men and brethren, what shall we do? Then Peter said unto them, Repent, and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the remission of sins... And with many other words did he testify and exhort, saying, Save yourselves from this untoward generation.
verses.context: Peter's Pentecost sermon contains the three elements: (1) gospel proclamation — prophecy fulfilled, Jesus approved by God, death, resurrection, exaltation (vv.14-36); (2) exposure — 'ye have taken, and by wicked hands have crucified' (v.23), 'whom ye have crucified' (v.36); (3) repentance/judgment call — 'Repent' (v.38), 'Save yourselves from this untoward generation' (v.40).
verses.notes: Acts 2:36 parsing: ginosketo (present imperative — let know assuredly), epoiesen (aorist — God made/appointed), estaurosate (aorist — you crucified). The exposure is devastating: the One you crucified, God made Lord and Christ. v.40 'save yourselves from this untoward generation' — the judgment warning element, echoing Noah (2 Pet 2:5).

verses.heading: Peter's temple sermon — gospel, exposure of denial, judgment warning
verses.kjv_text: And when Peter saw it, he answered unto the people, Ye men of Israel, why marvel ye at this?... The God of Abraham, and of Isaac, and of Jacob, the God of our fathers, hath glorified his Son Jesus; whom ye delivered up, and denied him in the presence of Pilate, when he was determined to let him go. But ye denied the Holy One and the Just, and desired a murderer to be granted unto you; And killed the Prince of life, whom God hath raised from the dead; whereof we are witnesses... Repent ye therefore, and be converted, that your sins may be blotted out... For Moses truly said unto the fathers, A prophet shall the Lord your God raise up unto you of your brethren, like unto me; him shall ye hear in all things whatsoever he shall say unto you. And it shall come to pass, that every soul, which will not hear that prophet, shall be destroyed from among the people.
verses.context: Peter's second sermon repeats the three-part pattern: (1) gospel — God glorified Jesus, raised him from the dead (vv.13,15,26); (2) exposure — 'ye delivered up and denied' the Holy One, killed the Prince of life (vv.13-15); (3) judgment warning — 'every soul which will not hear that prophet shall be destroyed' (v.23).
verses.notes: v.23 is an explicit judgment warning using Deut 18:19. The pattern is clear: gospel (what God has done) → exposure (what you have done) → judgment (what will happen if you refuse).

verses.heading: Paul at Antioch — gospel history, Israel's rejection, judgment warning
verses.kjv_text: Then Paul stood up, and beckoning with his hand said, Men of Israel, and ye that fear God, give audience. The God of this people of Israel chose our fathers, and exalted the people when they dwelt as strangers in the land of Egypt... Of this man's seed hath God according to his promise raised unto Israel a Saviour, Jesus: When John had first preached before his coming the baptism of repentance to all the people of Israel... Men and brethren, children of the stock of Abraham, and whosoever among you feareth God, to you is the word of this salvation sent. For they that dwell at Jerusalem, and their rulers, because they knew him not, nor yet the voices of the prophets which are read every sabbath day, they have fulfilled them in condemning him... And we declare unto you glad tidings, how that the promise which was made unto the fathers, God hath fulfilled the same unto us their children, in that he hath raised up Jesus again... Beware therefore, lest that come upon you, which is spoken of in the prophets; Behold, ye despisers, and wonder, and perish: for I work a work in your days, a work which ye shall in no wise believe, though a man declare it unto you.
verses.context: Paul at Antioch Pisidia follows the same pattern as Stephen: (1) gospel history — God chose Israel, raised David, sent Jesus (vv.16-25); (2) exposure — Jerusalem rulers condemned Jesus out of ignorance, fulfilled the prophets (vv.27-29); (3) judgment warning — 'Beware... lest that come upon you' (vv.40-41), quoting Hab 1:5.
verses.notes: v.32 euangelizometha (we declare unto you glad tidings) — middle voice of euangelizo. v.38 aphesis hamartion (forgiveness of sins) — the gospel content. vv.40-41 judgment warning using Habakkuk 1:5. This sermon is structurally parallel to Stephen's speech but from a different speaker to a different audience.

verses.heading: Jesus identifies John as Elijah who was to come
verses.kjv_text: And as they departed, Jesus began to say unto the multitudes concerning John, What went ye out into the wilderness to see? A reed shaken with the wind? But what went ye out for to see? A man clothed in soft raiment? behold, they that wear soft clothing are in kings' houses. But what went ye out for to see? A prophet? yea, I say unto you, and more than a prophet. For this is he, of whom it is written, Behold, I send my messenger before thy face, which shall prepare thy way before thee. Verily I say unto you, Among them that are born of women there hath not risen a greater than John the Baptist: notwithstanding he that is least in the kingdom of heaven is greater than he. And from the days of John the Baptist until now the kingdom of heaven suffereth violence, and the violent take it by force. For all the prophets and the law prophesied until John. And if ye will receive it, this is Elias, which was for to come.
verses.context: Jesus explicitly identifies John as the Elijah of Malachi 4:5-6. This establishes the Elijah chain: historical Elijah → John the Baptist → end-time Elijah (the three angels' messengers).
verses.notes: v.14 'this is Elias which was for to come' — Jesus' identification is conditional ('if ye will receive it') but explicit. This connects John's three-part ministry to the Elijah pattern documented in 3am-02 (Elijah at Carmel).

verses.heading: Angel's prophecy — John goes in spirit and power of Elijah
verses.kjv_text: For he shall be great in the sight of the Lord, and shall drink neither wine nor strong drink; and he shall be filled with the Holy Ghost, even from his mother's womb. And many of the children of Israel shall he turn to the Lord their God. And he shall go before him in the spirit and power of Elias, to turn the hearts of the fathers to the children, and the disobedient to the wisdom of the just; to make ready a people prepared for the Lord.
verses.context: The angel Gabriel's prophecy about John. 'In the spirit and power of Elijah' (en pneumati kai dynamei Heliou) directly links John to the Malachi 4:5-6 prophecy.
verses.notes: Parsing: proeleusetai (future middle — he will go before) en pneumati (G4151, dative — in spirit) kai dynamei (G1411, dative — and power) Heliou (G2243, genitive — of Elijah). epistrepsai (aorist infinitive — to turn) kardias (hearts) pateron (of fathers) epi tekna (upon children). The Elijah connection is divinely announced — not a later theological construction.

verses.heading: Day of Christ not yet — apostasy must come first
verses.kjv_text: Let no man deceive you by any means: for that day shall not come, except there come a falling away first, and that man of sin be revealed, the son of perdition; Who opposeth and exalteth himself above all that is called God, or that is worshipped; so that he as God sitteth in the temple of God, shewing himself that he is God.
verses.context: Paul explicitly warns that the day of Christ cannot come until the apostasy (apostasia) and man of sin are revealed. This shows apostolic awareness that the final judgment was reserved for a future period.
verses.notes: This verse supports Andrews' anticipation-vs-announcement distinction: Paul knew the judgment day was not imminent in his time. The NT preachers anticipated judgment; the first angel announces its arrival.

verses.heading: OT judgment scene — Ancient of Days, books opened
verses.kjv_text: I beheld till the thrones were cast down, and the Ancient of days did sit, whose garment was white as snow, and the hair of his head like the pure wool: his throne was like the fiery flame, and his wheels as burning fire. A fiery stream issued and came forth from before him: thousand thousands ministered unto him, and ten thousand times ten thousand stood before him: the judgment was set, and the books were opened.
verses.context: Daniel's vision of the heavenly judgment. This is the OT scene that Rev 14:7 announces has arrived — 'the hour of his judgment is come.' The NT preachers anticipated this; the first angel announces it.
verses.notes: The judgment was set (dina yatib) — the court convened. This is the judgment scene that was sealed until the time of the end (Dan 8:17, 12:4).

verses.heading: Sealed until time of the end — the vision pertains to the end
verses.kjv_text: So he came near where I stood: and when he came, I was afraid, and fell upon my face: but he said unto me, Understand, O son of man: for at the time of the end shall be the vision.
verses.context: Daniel's visions are explicitly sealed for 'the time of the end.' This explains why the NT preachers could anticipate but not announce the specific judgment hour.
verses.notes: The sealing of Daniel's prophecies is the key to Andrews' distinction: the NT speakers knew judgment was coming but the specific announcement was reserved.

verses.heading: Sealed book — knowledge increased at the time of the end
verses.kjv_text: But thou, O Daniel, shut up the words, and seal the book, even to the time of the end: many shall run to and fro, and knowledge shall be increased.
verses.context: Daniel told to seal the book until the time of the end. The unsealing corresponds to the first angel's announcement — the judgment hour has arrived.
verses.notes: The sealed-then-unsealed pattern explains the temporal distinction: NT preachers delivered the three-part pattern, but the specific announcement of 'the hour IS come' was reserved until the sealed prophecies were opened.

verses.heading: Jesus' gospel call — repent for the kingdom is at hand
verses.kjv_text: From that time Jesus began to preach, and to say, Repent: for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.
verses.context: Jesus takes up John's exact message. kerysso (G2784) + metanoeo (G3340) — the same vocabulary, the same call.
verses.notes: Supporting verse showing continuity between John's and Jesus' message.

verses.heading: Jesus' gospel summary — the time is fulfilled, repent and believe
verses.kjv_text: Now after that John was put in prison, Jesus came into Galilee, preaching the gospel of the kingdom of God, And saying, The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand: repent ye, and believe the gospel.
verses.context: Mark's summary of Jesus' message: kerysso the euangelion, the kairos is fulfilled, repent and believe.
verses.notes: kairos (G2540, appointed time) is pepleromenon (perfect passive — has been fulfilled). Jesus proclaims the time as arrived — anticipating the first angel's announcement at a later scale.

verses.heading: Nineveh's repentance as judgment against Israel
verses.kjv_text: The men of Nineveh shall rise in judgment with this generation, and shall condemn it: because they repented at the preaching of Jonas; and, behold, a greater than Jonas is here.
verses.context: Jesus uses Nineveh's response to Jonah as a judgment warning. kerygma (G2782) — preaching/proclamation. The three-part pattern is implicit: Jonah preached (gospel), Nineveh's wickedness was exposed, judgment was announced.
verses.notes: kerygma (G2782, 8 occurrences) — here used of Jonah's proclamation. Jesus invokes judgment comparison: if Nineveh repented at lesser preaching, this generation is under greater judgment.

verses.heading: Paul's kerygma summary — the gospel defined
verses.kjv_text: Moreover, brethren, I declare unto you the gospel which I preached unto you, which also ye have received, and wherein ye stand; By which also ye are saved, if ye keep in memory what I preached unto you, unless ye have believed in vain. For I delivered unto you first of all that which I also received, how that Christ died for our sins according to the scriptures; And that he was buried, and that he rose again the third day according to the scriptures:
verses.context: Paul defines the core kerygma: Christ died for sins, was buried, rose again. This is the gospel content that all the apostolic sermons share.
verses.notes: euangelion (G2098) — the gospel content defined. Paul claims this is received tradition ('delivered unto you... which I also received'). This establishes continuity of the gospel core across all NT speakers.

verses.heading: Apostolic kerygma continuity — we all preach the same
verses.kjv_text: Therefore whether it were I or they, so we preach, and so ye believed.
verses.context: Paul claims continuity with the broader apostolic kerygma — whether Paul or Peter or the other apostles, they all preach the same message.
verses.notes: keryssomen (we preach, G2784) — present tense. Dodd highlights this verse as proving the kerygma was consistent across all apostolic preachers. This supports the study's thesis of a recurring structural pattern.

verses.heading: Peter — ordained Judge of living and dead
verses.kjv_text: And he commanded us to preach unto the people, and to testify that it is he which was ordained of God to be the Judge of quick and dead.
verses.context: Peter's sermon at Cornelius' house includes judgment as integral to the gospel: Christ is appointed Judge.
verses.notes: kriten (judge) of zonton kai nekron (living and dead). Judgment is part of the apostolic commission — they were commanded to testify to Christ's role as Judge.

verses.heading: Gospel of the kingdom preached to all nations, then the end
verses.kjv_text: And this gospel of the kingdom shall be preached in all the world for a witness unto all nations; and then shall the end come.
verses.context: Jesus' prophecy of universal gospel proclamation before the end. The scope ('all nations') parallels Rev 14:6.
verses.notes: euangelion (G2098) + basileia (G932) + kerychthesetai (future passive of kerysso) + panta ta ethne (all nations). This prophecy bridges the apostolic preaching and the first angel's message.

verses.heading: Judgment seat of Christ — all must appear
verses.kjv_text: For we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ; that every one may receive the things done in his body, according to that he hath done, whether it be good or bad.
verses.context: Paul's statement that ALL must appear before Christ's judgment seat. Judgment is universal, not avoidable.
verses.notes: bema tou Christou (judgment seat of Christ). Paul teaches universal accountability — another expression of judgment as integral to the gospel.

verses.heading: Paul quotes Isa 52:7 as gospel
verses.kjv_text: And how shall they preach, except they be sent? as it is written, How beautiful are the feet of them that preach the gospel of peace, and bring glad tidings of good things!
verses.context: Paul quotes Isaiah 52:7, applying the basar (good tidings) passage to gospel preaching. The original context is deliverance from Babylon.
verses.notes: euangelizomenon (middle participle of euangelizo) — the good news proclaimer. Paul's use of Isa 52:7 as a gospel text connects the NT gospel vocabulary to the Babylonian deliverance context — the second angel territory.

verses.heading: John Reproves Herod — Exposure Extended to Political Corruption
verses.kjv_text: And many other things in his exhortation preached he unto the people. But Herod the tetrarch, being reproved by him for Herodias his brother Philip's wife, and for all the evils which Herod had done, Added yet this above all, that he shut up John in prison.
verses.context: John the Baptist confronts Herod about Herodias and all his evils — the exposure element extends beyond religious hypocrisy to political corruption, leading to imprisonment

greek_parsing.gloss: ἕτερα                ἕτερος          G2087    [A-APN] Acc Pl N
Pres Act Ptcp Nom Sg M

verses.notes: The exposure element of John's ministry was not limited to religious audiences. He confronted political power directly (Herod), paralleling the second angel's exposure of Babylon's political-religious corruption.

word_studies.summary: To announce good news, evangelize. 88% middle voice — the evangelist personally engages. Used in Luke 3:18 to label John's ENTIRE message (including judgment warnings) as gospel. The basar→euangelizo LXX bridge connects to OT good tidings.
word_studies.relevance: Luke 3:18 uses euangelizo (imperfect middle) to describe John's entire message including judgment warnings as gospel. This parallels Rev 14:6 where the judgment announcement IS the everlasting gospel.

word_studies.summary: The gospel, the good message. Used in Rev 14:6 as euangelion aionion (everlasting gospel), Rom 1:16 (gospel is power of God), Rom 2:16 (according to my gospel). The noun denotes the content of the proclamation.
word_studies.relevance: The key content word — what the first angel carries (euangelion aionion) and what Paul explicitly ties to judgment (kata to euangelion mou, Rom 2:16).

word_studies.summary: Judgment — a decision, tribunal, justice. From krino (G2919). Used in Rev 14:7 (hora tes kriseos — hour of judgment), Matt 23:33 (krisis of geenna). Bridges OT riyb/mishpat through the LXX.
word_studies.relevance: The central judgment term in the first angel's message. The riyb-to-krisis bridge (documented in 3am-02 and TR-riyb-krisis) means Rev 14:7 carries the full weight of the OT covenant lawsuit tradition.

word_studies.summary: To judge, to separate, to distinguish. Used in Acts 17:31 (krinen ten oikoumenen — judge the world), Rom 2:16 (God judges secrets), Rom 3:6 (how shall God judge the world).
word_studies.relevance: The verb form of the judgment concept. Acts 17:31 uses krino for Paul's judgment announcement at Athens; Rom 2:16 ties it to 'my gospel.'

word_studies.summary: To repent — to think differently afterward, to change one's mind for the better. First NT occurrence Matt 3:2 (John's first word). Central to every NT preaching instance. Heaviest concentration in Revelation (10 of 32 uses).
word_studies.relevance: Central to the gospel-call element of every NT preaching instance. Acts 17:30 commands 'all men everywhere to repent' — universal scope matching Rev 14:6.

word_studies.summary: Wrath — properly, desire extended to violent passion. Used in Matt 3:7 (flee from orge to come), Rom 1:18 (orge theou revealed), 1 Thess 1:10 (orge erchomene — coming wrath).
word_studies.relevance: The wrath vocabulary appearing in John's preaching (Matt 3:7), Paul's theology (Rom 1:18), and 1 Thess 1:10. Bridges OT chemah/H639 through LXX.

word_studies.summary: Fury, fierceness, wrath — from thyo (to rush/breathe hard). Distinguished from orge as more passionate eruption. 10 of 18 NT occurrences in Revelation. Rev 14:10 uses thumos tou theou (fury of God).
word_studies.relevance: Rev 14:10 thumos tou theou connects through the chemah-thumos bridge (documented in TR-chemah-thymos and 3am-02) to the OT wrath tradition.

word_studies.summary: To worship, to prostrate oneself in homage. Used in Rev 14:7 (proskynesate — worship the Creator) and Rev 14:9,11 (worship the beast). LXX renders Hebrew shachah (H7812).
word_studies.relevance: The worship polarity — proskyneo the Creator (first angel) vs. proskyneo the beast (third angel). The shachah-proskyneo bridge documented in TR-shachah-proskyneo.

word_studies.summary: To fear, to reverence. Used in Rev 14:7 as phobethete ton theon (fear God). Bridges OT yare through the LXX.
word_studies.relevance: The opening command of the first angel's message. The yare-phobeo bridge documented in TR-yare-phobeo.

word_studies.summary: To herald, to proclaim as a public crier. 74% active voice. First occurrence Matt 3:1 (John kerysso). Used of John, Jesus, Peter, Paul — the standard preaching verb. The herald speaks with delegated authority.
word_studies.relevance: The preaching verb used across all NT episodes — John (Matt 3:1), Jesus (Matt 4:17), apostles. Establishes continuity of the proclamation function.

word_studies.summary: Proclamation, what is preached. The noun form of kerysso. Used in Matt 12:41 of Jonah's preaching, 1 Cor 1:21, 15:14 of apostolic preaching. Dodd's analysis reconstructs this as the core apostolic message pattern.
word_studies.relevance: Dodd's kerygma analysis identifies the consistent pattern across apostolic preaching — the formal content that maps to the three-part structure.

word_studies.summary: Nation, people, race. Used in Rev 14:6 (panti ethnei — every nation), Acts 17:26 (all nations), Rom 1:5 (obedience among all nations). The universal scope of the gospel message.
word_studies.relevance: The universal scope marker — present in the first angel's message AND in all NT preaching episodes.

word_studies.summary: Idol — an image for worship. From eidos (form/appearance). Used in Acts 7:41 (golden calf), 1 Thess 1:9 (turned from idols), Acts 17:16 (city full of idols). The exposure object in the second-angel-parallel episodes.
word_studies.relevance: The exposure element across multiple episodes targets idolatry: Stephen exposes the golden calf (Acts 7:41), Paul confronts Athenian idols (17:16), Thessalonians turned FROM idols (1 Thess 1:9), Romans exposes idolatry (1:23).

word_studies.summary: To fall, to collapse. Used in Rev 14:8 (epesen Babylon — Babylon is fallen). The verb of Babylon's collapse.
word_studies.relevance: The verb of the second angel's announcement — Babylon IS FALLEN. The aorist tense declares the fall as accomplished fact.

word_studies.summary: Eternal, everlasting — pertaining to an age or ages. Used in Rev 14:6 (euangelion aionion — everlasting gospel). The gospel is not new but eternal.
word_studies.relevance: Modifies euangelion in Rev 14:6 — the everlasting gospel. The pattern being traced through the NT is not a new invention but the eternal gospel recurring.

word_studies.summary: To come out, to go forth. Used in Rev 18:4 (exelthate ex autes — come out of her). Bridges OT yatsa (H3318) — the separation-from-Babylon call.
word_studies.relevance: The yatsa-exerchomai bridge (documented in 3am-02) connects the OT come-out-of-Babylon call to Rev 18:4. The separation element is implicit in the second angel.

word_studies.summary: Glory — opinion, estimation, honor, splendor. Used in Rev 14:7 (dote auto doxan — give him glory) and Rom 1:23 (changed the glory of the incorruptible God). Glory given vs. glory exchanged.
word_studies.relevance: Rev 14:7 commands 'give glory to him.' Rom 1:23 describes the antiparallel: they 'changed the glory' of God into images. The glory exchange is the heart of the idolatry exposure.

word_studies.summary: Woe — a primary exclamation of grief. Matthew 14 occurrences, Luke 15, Revelation 14. In Matt 23 used as the prophetic denunciation formula against scribes and Pharisees (7x). In Revelation shifts to cosmic lament and eschatological events.
word_studies.relevance: Jesus' seven woes in Matt 23 use ouai as the prophetic denunciation formula — the systematic exposure element. The OT prophetic 'woe oracle' genre (Isa 5, Hab 2) is the precedent. Collocates with hupokrites (G5273) 7 times.

word_studies.summary: Hypocrite — from hupokrinomai (to act a part, to play a role). 15 of 20 occurrences in Matthew. The theatrical metaphor: outward performance concealing inner reality. Central to Jesus' seven-woe exposure in Matt 23.
word_studies.relevance: Central to the exposure element in Jesus' Matt 23 discourse. The false system Jesus exposes is religious hypocrisy — the Pharisaic system of outward conformity concealing inner corruption.

word_studies.summary: Stiff-necked — hapax legomenon in NT (Acts 7:51). Compound of skleros (hard) + trachelos (neck). LXX uses it 5x for Israel as a people collectively resistant to God. Stephen invokes the full covenant lawsuit tradition.
word_studies.relevance: Stephen's climactic accusation uses this Deuteronomic covenant term (Deut 9:6 LXX), invoking the riyb pattern documented in 3am-02. The stiff-necked designation connects Stephen's indictment to the OT covenant lawsuit tradition.

word_studies.summary: To fall against, to resist — compound of anti (against) + pipto (fall). Only 2 NT occurrences (both Acts). Present active indicative in Acts 7:51 — 'ye do always resist' — ongoing, continuous resistance against the Holy Spirit.
word_studies.relevance: The present tense in Acts 7:51 (antipiptete) indicates not a one-time act but a persistent pattern of resistance — 'ye do ALWAYS resist.' This reinforces Stephen's argument that Israel's rejection pattern is continuous.

word_studies.summary: Hour — literal clock hour, the decisive moment, appointed eschatological moment, unknown moment. In Rev 14:7 'the hour of his judgment is come' — the appointed eschatological moment has arrived. In John's Gospel, Jesus' 'hour' is the Christological turning point.
word_studies.relevance: Rev 14:7 uses hora as the appointed eschatological moment — 'the hour of his judgment IS COME.' The temporal distinction between NT preaching (judgment anticipated) and Rev 14:7 (judgment arrived) is encoded in this word.

word_studies.summary: God — the supreme divinity. Used throughout the study in Creator contexts (Acts 17:24, Rev 14:7) and judgment contexts (Rom 1:18, 2:16).
word_studies.relevance: The consistent God-concept across all episodes: Creator (Acts 17:24 = Rev 14:7), Judge (Rom 2:16, Acts 17:31), object of true worship.

word_studies.summary: Faith — persuasion, conviction, trust. Used in Rev 14:12 (pistis Iesou — faith of Jesus), Rom 1:17 (from faith to faith).
word_studies.relevance: Rev 14:12 pairs commandment-keeping with faith of Jesus. The result clause of the three-part pattern.

word_studies.summary: Power, ability, strength. Used in Rom 1:16 (dynamis theou — power of God unto salvation) and Luke 1:17 (dynamei Heliou — power of Elijah).
word_studies.relevance: Links the gospel to divine power (Rom 1:16) and John the Baptist to Elijah (Luke 1:17 — 'in the spirit and POWER of Elijah').

word_studies.summary: Day — a natural day, a period of time. Used in Acts 17:31 (estesen hemeran — appointed a day) and Rom 2:5 (hemera orges — day of wrath).
word_studies.relevance: The temporal marker in Acts 17:31 — God 'appointed a day' (estesen hemeran, aorist). This is the day anticipated by NT preachers; Rev 14:7 uses hora (hour) to announce it has arrived.

word_studies.summary: Kingdom — royal power, reign, kingdom. Used in Matt 3:2 (basileia ton ouranon — kingdom of heaven is at hand) and Matt 4:17.
word_studies.relevance: The gospel-call vocabulary of John and Jesus: 'the kingdom of heaven is at hand.' The kingdom announcement is the gospel element.

word_studies.summary: Sin — missing the mark, deviation from God's standard. Used throughout Romans 1-3 as the universal condition exposed.
word_studies.relevance: The universal condition that the exposure element reveals: all have sinned (Rom 3:23). The judgment is justified because sin is universal.

word_studies.summary: Righteousness — judicial equity, justness. Used in Rom 1:17 (righteousness of God revealed), Acts 17:31 (judge in righteousness), Acts 24:25 (reasoned of righteousness).
word_studies.relevance: Links the gospel (God's righteousness revealed, Rom 1:17) to judgment (judge in righteousness, Acts 17:31). Paul before Felix reasons of 'righteousness, temperance, judgment' — three connected concepts.

word_studies.summary: Condemnation — an adverse sentence. Only 3 occurrences (Rom 5:16, 5:18, 8:1). Distinguished from krisis (judicial process) — katakrima is the verdict itself.
word_studies.relevance: The distinction between krisis (process) and katakrima (adverse verdict) clarifies that believers face krisis but not katakrima (Rom 8:1) — judgment is evaluative, not automatically condemnatory.

word_studies.summary: To preach the gospel beforehand. Hapax in Gal 3:8 — 'the Scripture, foreseeing that God would justify the heathen through faith, preached before the gospel unto Abraham.'
word_studies.relevance: The gospel was preached BEFORE (pro-) to Abraham — supporting the thesis that the three-part pattern is the 'everlasting gospel,' not a new invention.
